core_intuitions: SIMT gives you a programmable machine and makes you manage the memory hierarchy; a systolic accelerator gives you a compiler and makes you accept its abstractions — this one contrast explains ~90% of the porting pain.

A systolic array does dense matmul only: weights sit stationary in a MAC grid, activations pump in from the left one column per clock, partial sums march downward and NEVER leave the array — almost no SRAM/register traffic, so huge FLOP/watt but rigid.
The roofline ridge point I* = peak FLOP/s / peak byte/s classifies every op: below I* it is memory-bound (rank chips by HBM bandwidth), above I* it is compute-bound (rank chips by peak TFLOPS).

Autoregressive decode is bandwidth-bound (stream the whole weight matrix from HBM per token, tiny per-token compute); training and prefill are compute-bound dense GEMMs — this single flip decides which chip wins.

Portability tracks philosophy: SIMT->SIMT (CUDA->AMD) is a translation problem (rename APIs, re-tune for 64-wide wavefronts); SIMT->systolic (TPU/Trainium/Gaudi) is a paradigm rewrite to graph-level array code owned by a compiler.

Pick a chip by three spec-sheet numbers gated in order: capacity FIRST (does the model + KV cache fit?), then bandwidth (decode) or TFLOPS (prefill/train) — capacity can put a 152 GB model on ONE 192 GB device instead of two 80 GB ones, deleting the decode-path all-reduce.

existing.name: accel-systolic-array-dataflow
existing.improvement: Renders correctly and legibly in both themes: weight-stationary cells, activations streaming left->right one column per clock, partial sums marching down to output columns, and a clear 'partial sums never leave the array -> almost no SRAM/register traffic' callout that nails the FLOP/watt intuition. Reading order and legend are clean. Nothing foundational is missing; leave as-is.

existing.name: accel-portability-spectrum
existing.issue: The axis is literally 'how much of the NVIDIA programming model survives', yet NVIDIA — the origin/zero-difficulty reference the whole spectrum is measured against — is absent. The leftmost box is AMD Instinct labelled 'EASIEST PORT', which reads as if AMD were the baseline rather than the first step away from it. A reader misses that the true zero point (100% survives) is the NVIDIA stack itself.
existing.improvement: Add an explicit NVIDIA CUDA anchor at the far-left origin of the axis (difficulty 0 / '100% of the model survives — you are already here'), visually distinct from the four porting-target boxes, so the spectrum reads as NVIDIA(origin) -> AMD(translation) -> Gaudi/Trainium/TPU(paradigm). Keep everything else; just seat the reference point that names the axis.

new_figures.name: accel-roofline-decode-vs-prefill
new_figures.concept: One roofline with the ridge point I* separating memory-bound decode (rank by HBM TB/s) from compute-bound prefill/training (rank by peak TFLOPS).
new_figures.rationale: This is the chapter's central decision tool: Step 2 tells you to 'use the ridge point' but no figure shows what that means. Placing decode on the sloped bandwidth region and prefill/training on the flat compute plateau makes the 'which spec-sheet number matters' rule visual and permanent — it is the reasoning that powers the whole 'pick a chip' thesis and the Interview Corner answer.
new_figures.placement: Section 'Reading a Spec Sheet to Pick Hardware' -> subsection 'Step 2: Is it compute-bound or bandwidth-bound? (roofline)' (place the marker right after that subsection's paragraph)
new_figures.brief: A classic roofline plot on log-log-feel axes (do not over-label the axes with numbers; this is a schematic). X-axis: 'Arithmetic intensity I (FLOPs per byte of HBM traffic)'. Y-axis: 'Attainable throughput (FLOP/s)'. Draw the roofline as two joined segments: (1) a diagonal slanted line rising from the lower-left = the BANDWIDTH ROOF (slope = peak HBM bandwidth), (2) a horizontal line at the top = the COMPUTE ROOF (peak TFLOPS). They meet at the ridge point; drop a light dashed vertical line from the ridge to the x-axis and label it 'I* = peak FLOP/s / peak byte/s (the ridge point)'. Shade/annotate the LEFT region under the diagonal as 'MEMORY-BOUND' and the RIGHT region under the horizontal as 'COMPUTE-BOUND'. Place two labelled operating points sitting ON the roofline: (a) a 'DECODE (one token)' marker low-left on the diagonal, with a callout 'streams the whole weight matrix per token, tiny compute -> low I -> limited by HBM bandwidth -> rank chips by TB/s'; (b) a 'PREFILL / TRAINING (dense GEMM)' marker up-right on the flat plateau, with a callout 'big statically-shaped matmuls -> high I -> limited by peak matmul -> rank chips by FP8/bf16 TFLOPS'. Optionally show a faint second, higher bandwidth roof (steeper diagonal) labelled 'more HBM bandwidth lifts the memory-bound region' to hint why a memory-rich chip helps decode — keep it subtle and clearly secondary. Use only small illustrative integers or none; invent NO benchmark numbers (no real TFLOPS/TB/s values). Theme-safe: use the palette's ink/accent tokens, no hardcoded reds/greens for the two regions — distinguish them by fill opacity + labels, not by named colors. Everything must sit inside the viewBox with clear reading order: title top, roofline center, two operating-point callouts not overlapping the curve. The static frame must fully convey: which side each workload lives on, and therefore which single spec-sheet number picks the chip.

new_figures.name: accel-capacity-decision-70b
new_figures.concept: The chapter thesis in miniature: a 152 GB (70 GB weights + 82 GB KV) decode-serving footprint needs two 80 GB devices (tensor-parallel split + per-layer all-reduce) but fits on one 192 GB device with no collective at all.
new_figures.rationale: The worked 70B example and the Interview Corner are the payoff of the whole chapter, but they are pure prose + arithmetic. A side-by-side memory-bar + decode-path diagram makes the causal chain visible — capacity forces device count, device count forces (or deletes) a per-layer all-reduce in the bandwidth-bound decode path — which is exactly why 'for decode you pick by capacity/bandwidth, not TFLOPS'. This is the figure that makes the thesis click.
new_figures.placement: Section 'Reading a Spec Sheet to Pick Hardware' -> admonition 'Worked example: serving a 70B model — capacity changes everything' (place the marker just before or right after that worked example)
new_figures.brief: A two-panel side-by-side comparison. TOP or LEFT of both panels: a single shared 'live footprint' stacked bar = 70 GB weights + 82 GB KV cache = ~152 GB total (label the two segments; use small illustrative integers exactly these, they come from the chapter's own worked example, not invented benchmarks). LEFT PANEL titled 'Two 80 GB devices (e.g. H100-class)': show the 152 GB footprint split across TWO device boxes each with an 80 GB capacity gauge — the model does not fit in one, so it is tensor-parallel sharded; draw a red-free but clearly-marked 'per-layer ALL-REDUCE over the interconnect' arrow looping between the two devices INSIDE the decode path, with a small caption 'decode pays a cross-device collective every layer'. RIGHT PANEL titled 'One 192 GB device (e.g. MI300X-class)': show the SAME 152 GB footprint sitting inside a single 192 GB capacity gauge with visible headroom, and a bold 'NO tensor-parallel collective in the decode path' badge; caption 'weights stream from one HBM stack; zero inter-device communication'. Under both panels a single takeaway strip: 'Decode is bandwidth-bound -> capacity that removes the all-reduce can beat higher aggregate FLOPS.' Keep device/vendor names generic-with-class-hint (write '80 GB device (H100-class)' / '192 GB device (MI300X-class)') so it reads as the method, not a datasheet. Show a small ceil-divide note near the left panel: 'ceil(152 / 80) = 2 devices' and near the right 'ceil(152 / 192) = 1 device'. Theme-safe: capacity gauges as fill-vs-outline (filled = used, outline = headroom), no hardcoded primary colors for 'good/bad'; distinguish the all-reduce arrow by weight/dash + label, not color alone. Everything inside the viewBox, two panels clearly separated, reading order: shared footprint bar -> left (2 devices, collective) -> right (1 device, none) -> takeaway strip. The static frame alone must convey the full causal chain.

new_figures.name: accel-simt-vs-systolic-silicon-budget
new_figures.concept: Why systolic beats SIMT on FLOP/watt for dense matmul: nearly all systolic silicon is MAC cells, while a SIMT core spends a large fraction on control logic, caches, and scheduling to stay general-purpose.
new_figures.rationale: The chapter asserts 'far higher FLOPs per watt and per transistor... because almost all the silicon is MAC units and almost none is control logic or caches' and frames the whole two-philosophies section on it, but nothing shows the trade. A die-area budget contrast makes the flexibility-vs-efficiency bet tangible and complements the existing dataflow figure (which shows HOW a systolic array works, not WHY it wins on efficiency / loses on flexibility).
new_figures.placement: Section 'Two Silicon Philosophies: Systolic Arrays vs SIMT' -> right after the 'GPUs give you a programmable machine... systolic accelerators give you a compiler' bolded sentence (before the roofline formula)
new_figures.brief: A side-by-side 'transistor budget' comparison of two stylized chip floorplans of equal total area. LEFT: 'SIMT core (NVIDIA/AMD)' — the square is divided into labelled regions of illustrative proportion: a modest 'Tensor/Matrix Cores (MACs)' block plus LARGE regions for 'programmable ALUs / registers', 'shared memory / L1', 'L2 cache', 'warp schedulers / instruction fetch / control'. Caption: 'general-purpose & programmable — you orchestrate the memory hierarchy by hand'. RIGHT: 'Systolic array (TPU/Trainium/Gaudi MME)' — the same square is almost entirely one dense grid labelled 'MAC cells (dense matmul)', with only a thin strip for 'vector unit (softmax/norm/elementwise)' and a tiny sliver for 'control / sequencer'. Caption: 'almost all silicon is MACs — high FLOP/watt, but dense-matmul only; a compiler must schedule everything'. Add a compact verdict bar spanning both: LEFT end 'more flexible (runs anything)', RIGHT end 'more efficient per watt/transistor (dense matmul only)', with an arrow. Use proportions only illustratively — no percentages presented as real specs, no invented numbers. Theme-safe: distinguish region types by fill opacity / hatching + labels, not by named primary colors; the MAC regions on both sides should share one visual token so the eye directly compares 'how much is MACs'. Keep both floorplans equal size for honest area comparison, all labels legible and non-overlapping inside the viewBox.

summary: Chapter is concept-rich and currently has two solid figures (systolic dataflow: keep; portability spectrum: refine by adding the missing NVIDIA origin that names its own axis). The two biggest un-served intuitions are the chapter's actual thesis: (1) the roofline classification that says decode->rank by bandwidth, prefill/training->rank by TFLOPS, and (2) the capacity-driven serving decision where a 152 GB footprint fits on one 192 GB device (no all-reduce) vs two 80 GB devices. Both proposed as priority-1 statics. A priority-3 SIMT-vs-systolic die-area-budget figure would make the flexibility-vs-efficiency bet tangible, complementing the existing dataflow figure.
